title: Rootless containers with pasta provide no per-container network attribution, making host security enforcement impossible
body:
Feature request description
---
Summary

-   When using rootless Podman with the default pasta networking backend, all outbound network connections from all containers are proxied through a single shared pasta process. 
- From the host's perspective, these connections are indistinguishable, it is impossible to attribute a network connection to the specific container that initiated it, either via kernel interfaces or the Podman API.
- There is no kernel-accessible mechanism to answer the attribution question. pasta's NAT table is opaque userspace state.

---                                                             
  Observed behavior                                                                                                                                                                                
                                                                  
  A single pasta process serves all rootless containers for a user:
                                                                                                                                                                                                   
  /usr/bin/pasta --config-net --netns /run/user/1000/containers/networks/rootless-netns/rootless-netns ...
                                                                                                                                                                                                   
  This is a shared netns, not per-container. When any container makes an outbound connection:                                                                                                      
                                                                                                                                                                                                   
  1. The packet flows: container netns → rootless-netns → pasta → eno1                                                                                                                             
  2. By the time it reaches the host interface, the source is pasta (uid 1000)
  3. The original container's identity is gone                                                                                                                                                     
  4. pasta maintains its own internal connection table in userspace memory — not accessible via any kernel interface
                                                                                                                                                                                                   
  Even podman inspect cannot answer: "which container made this specific TCP connection to 8.8.8.8:443 at 14:03:22?"


### Suggest potential solution

One or more of the following would resolve this:

  1. pasta exposes a real-time attribution socket/API — mapping host-socket ↔ guest-ip:port ↔ container-id — that Podman/netavark surfaces. This would allow security tools to query connection    
  attribution.
  2. Per-container pasta instances — one pasta process per container instead of one shared process per user. This restores the 1:1 mapping and makes container attribution trivial from the host.  
  3. Podman provides a connection attribution API — even if pasta remains shared, Podman could maintain the mapping internally and expose it via the existing Podman socket API as a real-time     
  event stream.                                                                                                                                                                                    
4. Documentation clearly states this limitation — if none of the above is feasible, the security documentation for rootless Podman should explicitly state that host-level per-container network 
  monitoring and enforcement is not possible with the pasta backend.

### Have you considered any alternatives?

A clear and concise description of any alternative solutions or features you've considered.

### Additional context

Add any other context or screenshots about the feature request here.

comments.body:
> * When using rootless Podman with the default pasta networking backend, all outbound network connections from all containers are proxied through a single shared pasta process.
> 
> * From the host's perspective, these connections are indistinguishable, it is impossible to attribute a network connection to the specific container that initiated it, either via kernel interfaces or the Podman API.
> 
> * There is no kernel-accessible mechanism to answer the attribution question. pasta's NAT table is opaque userspace state.

Well that is just the intended design, all container communication goes through the rootless-netns which then gets proxied to the host https://github.com/containers/podman/discussions/22943#discussioncomment-9795883
If you want one pasta process per container you gets this already with `--network=pasta` (which is the default even)

I don't see why we should introduce complex APIs to monitor connecting state, that would require custom tolling that IMO basically nobody would use ever.

The rootless logic mirrors what root does in the rootless-netns, so if your claim is you can monitor the rootful kernel state then well you can monitor the rootless state the exact same way just in a different netns, i.e. `podman unshare --rootless-netns conntrack -L -n`.
In fact the NAT is done by the kernel there before the traffic is send into the pasta tap interface so pasta does not even know the source ip of the container at this point. And in general pasta has no idea that there are multiple containers nor its ids so it cannot expose such information.


cc @sbrivio-rh (in case you wan to add something)
